name: Mesa NIR
summary: Mesa's GPU shader intermediate representation: SSA-based, used as the optimisation substrate for Vulkan/OpenGL drivers across the Mesa stack. SPIR-V → NIR → vendor-specific lowering. Where SPIR-V is the portable interchange, NIR is the optimisation IR each Mesa driver works with.

sections.title: Overview
sections.content:
**Mesa NIR** (the New Intermediate Representation, despite the name now being old) is Mesa's GPU shader IR. SSA-based and used as the optimisation substrate for Vulkan/OpenGL drivers across the Mesa stack. The flow is: SPIR-V (or GLSL via glslang) → NIR → vendor-specific lowering → target GPU ISA.

Where SPIR-V is the portable interchange that frontends emit and drivers consume, NIR is the *optimisation IR* each Mesa driver works with. NIR's design supports common GPU shader optimisations: dead-code elimination, copy propagation, loop unrolling, vectorisation/devectorisation, and the texture-coordinate manipulation that GPU compilers spend most of their time on.

sections.title: Design and pipeline position
sections.content:
NIR sits between language frontends (GLSL, SPIR-V) and the vendor-specific backends (Intel, AMD, freedreno, Mali). Frontends translate to NIR; optimisation passes are written against NIR; backends lower NIR to their target ISA.

The shape is similar to LLVM IR but specialised for GPU shaders: NIR supports vector operations natively, has primitives for texture operations, and includes the kinds of metadata GPU compilers need (uniform/varying distinctions, texture binding indices). It is not designed for general-purpose CPU code.
